raw_case:
GIST-ery solved: story of an intestinal mass in a young lady


Abstract

A 19-year-old woman presented with abdominal pain and a palpable mass, initially suspected to be a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) based on imaging. Surgical excision revealed a sclerotic spindle cell neoplasm with minimal cytological atypia, but immunohistochemistry (IHC) was negative for GIST-specific markers. The pan-negative IHC profile, along with calcification foci and low Ki67 index (<1%), led to a diagnosis of calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT). This case highlights the importance of precise diagnostic evaluation and consideration of rare entities like CFT. Comprehensive histopathological evaluation and IHC are essential diagnostic tools, as they can distinguish between GIST and CFT, leading to accurate treatment and patient management. This case underscores the value of thorough pathological assessment in resolving diagnostic challenges.

Introduction

Calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT) is a rare benign mesenchymal neoplasm characterized by distinctive histopathological features, including hyalinized collagenous fibrous tissue with psammomatous and dystrophic calcifications [1]. In the past, CFT has been recorded in the gastrointestinal tract, abdominal cavity, pleura and neck in addition to its original description as a subcutaneous soft tissue entity [2]. Accurate diagnosis through detailed histopathological and immunohistochemical evaluation is crucial, as its presentation can mimic more common neoplasms such as gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs).

Case report

A 19-year-old woman presented with a 15-day history of severe abdominal pain and a palpable mass, with no history of hematemesis or perforation. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography revealed a well-defined solid lesion with calcification arising from the terminal ileal wall, prompting suspicion of a GIST (Fig. 1). Surgical excision of the terminal ileum, caecum, and appendix was performed for further assessment.The surgical specimen revealed a nodular mass measuring 8.3 × 5.6 × 6.8 cm in the terminal ileum, characterized by its distinct non-encapsulated spherical to lobulated shape with variable calcifications. Sectioning revealed a uniform, partially granular grey-white inner surface ranging in texture from firm to rubbery (Fig. 2a).Microscopic examination of the tissue sections revealed a predominantly bland spindle cell lesion that was very fibrous and had a large number of dystrophic and psammomatous calcifications (Fig. 1b and c). Inflammatory cells such plasma cells, were present. At first, the features suggested a sclerosing GIST due to the presence of thin-walled prominent blood vessels with perivascular edema and lymphocyte aggregation suggestive of a spindle cell neoplasm. However, the absence of nuclear hypochromasia and minimal cytological atypia called for further investigation.Immunohistochemical staining was performed to confirm the diagnosis. CD117 (KIT), DOG1, CD34, S100, Beta-Catenin, Desmin, and SMA were negative. This pan-negative IHC profile, combined with the presence of foci of calcification within the lesion, led to the diagnosis of CFT. The Ki67 proliferation rate was low (<1%), confirming the benign nature of the tumor.

Discussion

CFT is an extremely rare benign tumor originating from fibroblasts, first identified in 1988 by Rosenthal et al. as a ‘Childhood fibrous tumor with psammoma bodies’ in peripheral soft tissues [3]. Later, in 1993, Fetsch et al. renamed it calcifying fibrous pseudotumor [4]. This tumor exhibits a wide range of anatomical locations and can mimic various spindle cell tumors. Although its exact etiopathogenesis remains unclear, potential associations include trauma, previous surgeries, and chronic infections [5]. CFT typically affects soft tissues, such as subcutaneous extremities and neck, but can also occur in organs like the pleura, lung, and adrenal gland [6]. It affects individuals of any age, with a median age of 3 years for soft tissue tumors and 43 years for visceral tumors, showing no gender predilection [7]. Gastric CFT is an exceptionally rare mesenchymal benign tumor with unknown pathogenesis, often misdiagnosed as common spindle cell lesions like leiomyoma and gastrointestinal stromal tumors [8]. Macroscopically, CFT appears well-circumscribed, unencapsulated, and solid, with a grey-whitish, gritty texture [9]. Microscopically, it features bland spindle cells, hyalinized collagenous tissue, psammomatous calcifications, and chronic inflammation, including plasma cells and persistent lymphoplasmacytic inflammation [6]. Notably, gastrointestinal tract CFTs differs from soft tissue CFTs by having more perivascular lymphocytes and lymphoid cuffs. Lesional cells express vimentin and Factor XIIIa but not actin, desmin, S100, or cytokeratins, with variable CD34 positivity [10].Imaging investigations of CFTs generally show a well-circumscribed mass with dispersed calcifications, which is a good representation of their histological nature. When planning surgery, CT and MRI are crucial, as CT is especially helpful in identifying regions of early mineralization that other imaging modalities could miss [1, 11].The main consideration in the differential diagnosis in our case was GIST and desmoid fibromatosis.GISTs are the most prevalent mesenchymal tumors in the GI tract, potentially mimicking CFTs in any luminal GI location, particularly in cases where CFTs lack a prominent inflammatory component. GISTs predominantly occur in the stomach, with rare instances reported in the esophagus [11]. Unlike CFTs, GISTs tend to affect younger individuals [9]. Similar to inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor (IMT), GISTs are characterized by the absence of calcifications. Typically, GISTs exhibit c-kit and PDGFRA mutations, accompanied by positive staining for CD117 and DOG1 [12].-Desmoid fibromatosis is a tumor primarily located in deep soft tissues, notorious for its high recurrence rate. It is distinguished by the proliferation of homogeneous spindle cells in long fascicles within a collagenous stroma. In contrast to CFT, desmoid fibromatosis usually lacks calcifications and displays positive immunohistochemical staining for nuclear beta-catenin, setting it apart from CFT [10].Several other conditions can present similarly to CFT, making differential diagnosis challenging [10]:- Calcifying aponeurotic fibroma (CAF): A paediatric condition that mostly affects the hands and feet and is more common in the distal extremities. This is a biphasic fibroblastic tumour that has fibrocartilaginous nodules and spindled fibroblasts. Compared to CFT, CAF has a greater local recurrence rate and is typically less well-circumscribed. The typical location and cartilaginous component aid in differentiating CAF from CFT.- IMT: Previously believed to indicate a late stage of CFT or occur in conjunction with CFT, this condition is frequently observed in children and young people. As of right now, CFT and IMT are classified differently by the WHO. Histologically, IMT appears as spindle cell proliferation with a noticeable lymphoplasmacytic infiltration; calcifications are rarely observed and the condition is typically more cellular. Unobserved in CFT, an ALK rearrangement is seen in up to 50% of IMTs.- Nodular Fasciitis: A benign and self-limiting cell proliferation of myofibroblasts and fibroblasts. Its rapid growth, high cellularity, and greater mitotic activity can make it resemble sarcomas. It demonstrates extravasated red blood cells, spindle cells with elongated nuclei, inflammatory cells, and mitotic activity along with focal myxoid areas histologically. There is no description of calcifications. In order to differentiate nodular fasciitis from CFT, smooth muscle actin is frequently positive in this condition. A distinctive t(17;22) translocation that results in a MYH9::UPS6 fusion is commonly seen in nodular fasciitis.- Solitary fibrous tumor (SFT): SFTs are frequently more cellular with many branching or ‘staghorn’ vessels and lack calcifications. CFT and SFT both express vimentin, whereas CD34 is diffuse positive in SFT but variable in CFT. SFTs express STAT6 and CD99 in contrast to CFTs, due to a rearrangement of the NAB2::STAT6 gene.- Leiomyomas share similarities with CFTs in their spindle cell composition and lack of nuclear atypia, and may also exhibit a hyalinized stroma [3]. However, leiomyomas are distinguishable by their characteristic cigar-shaped nuclei and more eosinophilic cytoplasm compared to CFTs. Additionally, leiomyomas typically express markers of smooth muscle differentiation, such as desmin and smooth muscle actin, unlike most CFTs [12].The primary goal of treating intestinal CFT is to alleviate symptoms like obstruction and intussusception. Segmental resection is the preferred treatment approach, although enucleation has also been performed. CFT is considered a benign entity, and local removal is generally curative. However, rare recurrences have been observed in extra-gastrointestinal lesions [5].An area of research is the presence of increase in IgG4:IgG ratios in the plasma cell aggregates studied in some CFT cases, pointing to a possible link between CFT and immunoglobulin G4-related illness (IgG4-RD). IgG4-positive plasma cells, a strong lymphoplasmacytic infiltration, and increased serum IgG4 are the hallmarks of IgG4-RD, a fibroinflammatory illness. However, serum IgG levels are not constantly available for assessment, and not all cases of CFT show a raised IgG4:IgG ratio. To better understand the connection between IgG4-RD and CFT, more study is required. [10, 11].

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 19-year-old woman  
- **Chief Complaint:** Abdominal pain and a palpable mass  
- **History of Present Illness:** 15-day history of severe abdominal pain and a palpable abdominal mass. No history of hematemesis or perforation.  
- **Past Medical History:** None provided  
- **Personal History:** None provided  
- **Family History:** None provided  
- **Physical Examination:** Not detailed in the report, only mentioned "palpable abdominal mass."  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - Imaging: Contrast-enhanced CT showed a well-defined solid lesion with calcification in the terminal ileal wall, arousing suspicion of GIST.  
  - Surgical findings: Nodular mass (8.3 × 5.6 × 6.8 cm) in the terminal ileum with calcification, grey-white inner surface, firm to rubbery texture.  
  - Microscopic findings: Bland spindle cell lesion with psammomatous and dystrophic calcifications, chronic lymphoplasmacytic inflammation, and minimal cytological atypia.  
  - Immunohistochemistry (IHC): Negative for CD117 (KIT), DOG1, CD34, S100, Beta-Catenin, Desmin, and SMA. Low Ki67 proliferation index (<1%).  

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Suspicions:**  
   - The clinical presentation and imaging findings prompted suspicion of gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST). Histological findings such as spindle cell neoplasms and prominent blood vessels reinforced the suspicion of GIST.

2. **Step 1: Exclusion of GIST:**  
   - GISTs typically exhibit cytological atypia, hypochromasia, and positive staining for CD117, DOG1, and CD34, none of which were observed. Additionally, GIST generally lacks calcifications, unlike this lesion.  

3. **Step 2: Consideration and Exclusion of Other Spindle Cell Tumors:**  
   - Immunohistochemical testing ruled out other differential diagnoses:  
     - **Desmoid Fibromatosis:** Typically positive for nuclear beta-catenin; lacks calcifications.  
     - **Calcifying Aponeurotic Fibroma (CAF):** More common in distal extremities and features cartilaginous components, which were absent here.  
     - **Inflammatory Myofibroblastic Tumor (IMT):** Shows higher cellularity and ALK rearrangement, which were not observed.  
     - **Nodular Fasciitis:** Typically associated with positive staining for smooth muscle actin and lacks calcifications.  
     - **Solitary Fibrous Tumor (SFT):** Features staghorn blood vessels, and shows diffuse positivity for CD34 and expression of STAT6, neither of which was present.  
     - **Leiomyoma:** Distinctive cigar-shaped nuclei and smooth muscle differentiation markers (desmin and SMA) were absent.  

4. **Step 3: Diagnosis of Calcifying Fibrous Tumor (CFT):**  
   - Based on microscopic features (bland spindle cells, hyalinized collagenous tissue, psammomatous calcifications, and chronic inflammation) and the pan-negative IHC profile, CFT was diagnosed. Low Ki67 confirmed its benign nature.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT), a rare benign mesenchymal neoplasm. This diagnosis was supported by histological findings (spindle cells, psammomatous calcifications), the pan-negative IHC profile, and the low Ki67 proliferation index. The exclusion of other spindle cell neoplasms through IHC and histopathological features solidified the diagnosis.

---

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 19-year-old woman with a 15-day history of severe abdominal pain and a palpable mass. The CT scan found a solid lesion in the terminal ileum with calcification, initially suspected as GIST. But the IHC results are negative for CD117 and DOG1, which are typically positive in GISTs. So that's conflicting.

First, GISTs are common in the stomach and small intestine, but they usually express KIT (CD117) and DOG1. The negativity here makes me think of other spindle cell tumors. The lesion is in the terminal ileum, so maybe other mesenchymal tumors? Let's consider alternatives.

The microscopic findings show bland spindle cells, psammomatous calcifications, and chronic inflammation. Psammoma bodies can be seen in things like neuroendocrine tumors or some sarcomas. But the IHC is negative for S100 (rules out neural tumors like schwannoma), CD34 (another marker sometimes in GISTs), and SMA (smooth muscle markers). Desmin is negative too, so not a leiomyoma or leiomyosarcoma.

The Ki67 is low, suggesting a low proliferation rate. So maybe a benign or low-grade tumor. What about inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor (IMT)? These can present as spindle cell lesions with inflammation. IMTs often express ALK, but the IHC here doesn't mention ALK status. However, some IMTs are ALK-negative. Another possibility is a desmoid tumor, which is a type of fibromatosis. Desmoids can be positive for Beta-Catenin, but here Beta-Catenin is negative.

Calcifications are present. Psammomatous calcifications are often associated with neuroendocrine tumors or meningiomas, but the location here is intestinal. Maybe a calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT)? CFTs are rare benign tumors with dense collagen, spindle cells, and calcifications. They often present with chronic inflammation and are negative for CD117, CD34, etc. The Ki67 would be low in CFTs too. That fits the IHC profile here.

Another thought: Gastrointestinal schwannoma? But S100 is negative. Leiomyoma? SMA and Desmin are negative. So probably not. The calcifications and spindle cells with low Ki67 point towards CFT. Also, the age is young, and CFTs can occur in younger patients. The location in the terminal ileum is a bit unusual, but possible. The surgical findings describe a firm, rubbery mass with calcification and grey-white surface, which aligns with CFT's gross features. The IHC markers all negative except for the low Ki67. So putting it all together, the diagnosis is likely Calcifying Fibrous Tumor.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The initial suspicion of GIST based on imaging is challenged by negative CD117 and DOG1 IHC, which are critical markers for GIST. This necessitates considering alternative spindle cell neoplasms.  
<step 2> The presence of psammomatous and dystrophic calcifications, bland spindle cells, and chronic inflammation points toward benign or low-grade lesions. Psammomatous calcifications are uncommon in GISTs but are characteristic of entities like calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT).  
<step 3> Negative IHC for S100, CD34, SMA, Desmin, Beta-Catenin, and lack of cytological atypia exclude other differentials (e.g., schwannoma, leiomyoma, desmoid tumor, neuroendocrine tumor). The low Ki67 (<1%) further supports a benign process.  
<step 4> Calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT) aligns with the histologic features (spindle cells, calcifications, lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate) and IHC profile (pan-negative for typical markers, low proliferation index). The terminal ileum location, while rare, is documented in CFT cases.  

### Answer:
Calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT) of the terminal ileum

reasoning_eval.step: The initial suspicion of GIST based on imaging is challenged by negative CD117 and DOG1 IHC, which are critical markers for GIST. This necessitates considering alternative spindle cell neoplasms.

The presence of psammomatous and dystrophic calcifications, bland spindle cells, and chronic inflammation points toward benign or low-grade lesions. Psammomatous calcifications are uncommon in GISTs but are characteristic of entities like calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT).

Negative IHC for S100, CD34, SMA, Desmin, Beta-Catenin, and lack of cytological atypia exclude other differentials (e.g., schwannoma, leiomyoma, desmoid tumor, neuroendocrine tumor). The low Ki67 (<1%) further supports a benign process.

Calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT) aligns with the histologic features (spindle cells, calcifications, lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate) and IHC profile (pan-negative for typical markers, low proliferation index). The terminal ileum location, while rare, is documented in CFT cases.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial suspicion of GIST based on clinical presentation and imaging, reinforced by histological findings of spindle cell neoplasms and prominent blood vessels.
Step 2: Exclusion of GIST due to absence of cytological atypia, hypochromasia, and positive staining for CD117, DOG1, and CD34, along with the presence of calcifications.
Step 3: Exclusion of desmoid fibromatosis due to lack of nuclear beta-catenin and absence of calcifications.
Step 4: Exclusion of calcifying aponeurotic fibroma due to absence in distal extremities and lack of cartilaginous components.
Step 5: Exclusion of inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor due to lower cellularity and absence of ALK rearrangement.
Step 6: Exclusion of nodular fasciitis due to absence of positive staining for smooth muscle actin and lack of calcifications.
Step 7: Exclusion of solitary fibrous tumor due to absence of staghorn blood vessels, diffuse positivity for CD34, and expression of STAT6.
Step 8: Exclusion of leiomyoma due to absence of distinctive cigar-shaped nuclei and smooth muscle differentiation markers (desmin and SMA).

Step 9: Diagnosis of calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT) based on microscopic features (bland spindle cells, hyalinized collagenous tissue, psammomatous calcifications, and chronic inflammation), pan-negative IHC profile, and low Ki67 proliferation index confirming its benign nature.
Step 10: Final diagnosis: Calcifying fibrous tumor (CFT), a rare benign mesenchymal neoplasm, supported by histological and immunohistochemical findings.
